He had a schedule that looked like he didn't have a spare minute. I mean, he had an elegant way of phrasing going to the bathroom so he could list it on his schedule, you know, and publish his schedule. And I used to do a lot more of it. See, I don't care as much now, but so client work days showed up on a schedule with the client name like the consulting days do. And so the whole schedule looked like packed to the gills. Sure, that's exactly why. Because the less room in the schedule there is, the more somebody wants to get in the schedule. Plus, it's a way of pronouncing this entire, you know, position. I just, I had a very interesting thing just happen. I had a guy call. I did work for these two guys 15 years ago. They own new visions, a multi-level company that was in the Dead Doctors Don't Lie mess, the Barracos. And we go back a long way. I borrowed, I used to borrow their father's folding chairs for my meetings at my self-improvement center. And their father was a Diamond Director distributor on Amway. And these guys were kids, you know. So there's a lot of kind of familiarity, you know, breeds contempt kind of things. And the Dead Doctors Don't Lie stuff, when I did two tapes for them however many years ago, that was. My fees were, of course, a lot less, you know, than they are now. And so one of the two brothers, B.K., wants to, he wants to direct market their weight loss stuff. And so they came to me week before last. And the first thing he did is he had somebody contact the office, not identifying that they were with new visions of Barraco at all, you know. Actually another copywriter. And I've got a client who might want Dan to do some things and is he available and so on. And then they sent a gift. And then he finally called Vicki. And it showed up on a call log. And of course I recognized the name and kind of bumped them up and gave them a phone appointment pretty quick. But he started the phone call with saying, you know, I'd pay attention, I'd been watching you. I haven't looked to see if they're a member or not. And I am aware that you're not taking any new clients. It's very hard to get you and you're going to cost a hell of a lot more than you cost us the last time. But let me tell you why I would like you to do this, right? Now that's a nice place to start. Say, if you want to, if you want the work, it's a nice place to start, you know. But it doesn't happen by accident. It happens because of all this stuff, you know. And so, you know, again, I used to resist it and now I recognize that it's part and parcel of the business that we're in. And it is my observation that most people under do it. If you go back and you read all the Hills old stuff, his newsletters and his magazines and stuff, he definitely didn't under do it. He over did it, if anything. Atlas. I was, I gave you the evil Keneval article. I mean, here's P.T. Barnum with a motorcycle, you know, is really what he was. And actually, more of his stunts failed than succeeded. The big one was set up to fail. And it got more pressed by failing than it did by succeeding. And this guy had nothing to sell. You know, there's nothing there. You just promote yourself to be yourself, sort of an early version of Paris Hilton, if you will. You wouldn't find the comparison flattering. But if you stop to think about it, that, you know, there's a lot of people in this business today of just being who they are and making money by being it. But they can't be subtle about it, you know. So I would encourage you to work on this, to do more with it than you do. The corollary, page 139, is a really cool principle. And this is called the principle of unique importance. And this is about taking whatever it is that you do. Whatever it is that you're about. And making it of much greater epic importance than it actually is. Because truth be told, damn few things are of epic importance. And every big success in the success industry has been about this. And so it has been somewhat Barnum-like pronouncements, creating proprietary language. Not being afraid to say that the kettlebell is the, you know, fitness breakthrough that ends all other fitness products on the planet now and forever. I mean, not being afraid to declare your thing has the thing of all things. And again, if you go back to all the classics, so if you again look at Hill, you look at Maltz, you look at Carnegie, you look at Peel, you look at these books that have stayed alive, won't die for two decades, three decades, four decades, five decades. Virtually entirely by word of mouth, there's nothing else driving their sales. And compare that to the lifespan of most books, period. There's clearly something they did that most don't. And it is this, more than it is anything else. So I gave you a few examples here, but examples are proliferate if you go look for them. And again, it is really about not like pulling your punches, not like declaring your thing good, but not extraordinary. Not being willing to declare your thing unique, even though intellectually we know it ain't unique. And in every category, if you start looking for the unique, you go back a long, long, long, long, long, long, long, long, long way in the history links in that category to try and find it. Undoubtedly somebody in the 1600s was, you know, filling up balls with sand or something and, you know, essentially doing the same damn thing. But that doesn't really matter. And so this principle of unique importance is about language and it is about proclamation. And it is in a sense about being over the top. You know, if you think about just simple things like Barnum and Bailey, the greatest show on earth. Disney, the happiest place on earth. I mean, these are proclamation statements. You know, Trump is to the point where people like kind of make fun of it because everything is the greatest project ever and the biggest building ever and the highest price ever and the, but that works. You know, I mean, and it works for him. And he's to the point, it's clearly ingrained in his behavior. He's not even doing it consciously anymore. He just does it with everything. So this is a real important thing to sort of check your writing about to see if you are going this far or if you're stopping short. And just a handful of examples are enough to now go compare them to what you've written about your thing and see, you know, whether you're going this far. If you think about, which it's a mute point example because you're not having any trouble selling it, but like if you think about your summit. I mean, that is a radical revolutionary invention, something that's never been done before and could never be done by anybody else and yada, yada, yada. And you know, you're probably not going as far as you could in describing it in that way. You know, that no one else could gather this collection of insiders, producers and so forth. And so if you go now and you look at all of your writing about yourselves, you the same, see, you're the same thing. You and I've had this Warren Buffett conversation. You know, I mean, the main reason everybody knows who Warren Buffett is, even, you know, cooks and maids and, you know, and view him as the world's greatest investor is because Warren Buffett has made a point of telling everybody he's the world's greatest investor.
